role_hints.role: Geoscientists and geophysicists (interpretation, QC, survey design)
role_hints.oews_basis: role_mix is 4-digit 5413 (engineering-services dominated) and does not list this code's core occupations — 19-2042 Geoscientists and geophysical specialists sit in the unlisted tail; hypothesis for the 6-digit code: est. ~20-30% of wage bill
role_hints.note: Seismic interpretation, velocity model building, anomaly picking and report writing are already heavily ML-assisted and are the most AI-exposed tasks; survey design judgment, client-facing interpretation calls and professional sign-off persist. The run should verify the actual occupation mix at 6-digit before anchoring on the 4-digit record.

role_hints.role: Field acquisition crews and equipment operators
role_hints.oews_basis: not in the listed top-10 (4-digit artifact); vibroseis/marine/airborne crew occupations sit in the tail — hypothesis est. ~20-30%
role_hints.note: Physical data acquisition — land crews, marine streamers, airborne gravity/magnetics, drone magnetometry. Autonomous nodes and drones augment but crews, permitting and logistics remain physical work.

role_hints.oews_basis: 15-1252 Software Developers 3.1% in the record plus processing geophysicists in the unlisted tail, est. ~10-15% total
role_hints.note: Denoising, migration, stacking and QC pipelines are algorithmic already; AI collapses the remaining manual parameter-tuning and QC labor. This is the most directly automatable cost line.

role_hints.oews_basis: 11-1021 General & Operations Managers 5.7% + 11-9041 Architectural & Engineering Managers 5.5%
role_hints.note: Client relationships with oil & gas / mining / infrastructure buyers persist; internal reporting, bidding paperwork and resourcing automate.
Permitting packages, HSE documentation, crew scheduling and client reporting automate substantially; field coordination and claims judgment persist.

role_hints.role: Engineers and architects listed in the 4-digit record
role_hints.oews_basis: 17-2051 Civil Engineers 12.8% + 17-1011 Architects 5.8% + 17-2141 Mechanical 3.8% + 17-2071 Electrical 3.0% + 17-3011 Drafters 3.3% + 47-4011 Inspectors 2.1% — almost certainly contamination from 541330/541310 firms in the 4-digit OEWS cell
role_hints.note: Treat these shares as an artifact of the 4-digit role_mix, not this code's structure — geophysical firms employ few civil engineers or architects. Reallocate to geoscience and field roles.

role_hints.oews_basis: long tail of office/admin occupations, est. remainder ~10-15%
role_hints.note: Invoicing, data delivery logistics, proposal and contract admin — near-fully automatable.

source_hints.source: Society of Exploration Geophysicists (SEG) publications and annual meeting proceedings
source_hints.why: Primary venue for ML-in-seismic-interpretation adoption evidence — how far AI processing/interpretation has actually penetrated commercial work.

source_hints.source: EAGE (European Association of Geoscientists and Engineers) publications
source_hints.why: Parallel technical-adoption evidence for AI in geophysical processing and interpretation.

source_hints.source: TGS and Viridien (formerly CGG) investor reports
source_hints.why: The listed seismic-data majors' filings give segment margins, multi-client library economics and acquisition activity — top-of-market comps for exit_mult; TGS-PGS merger (2024) frames the consolidation endgame.

source_hints.source: EnerGeo Alliance (formerly IAGC, International Association of Geophysical Contractors)
source_hints.why: Trade association of geophysical/energy-geoscience contractors — market structure and member-firm context.

source_hints.source: SEG / state geoscience licensure board rosters (e.g. Texas Board of Professional Geoscientists)
source_hints.why: Closest available proxy for owner demographics in small geophysical firms; no dedicated succession survey is known for this code.
Demand for geophysical services tracks exploration spending — needed to judge whether 2022-receipts-era revenue is cyclically representative.

capture_questions: Geophysical work is sold as competitive day-rate/project bids to oil & gas, mining and infrastructure clients with professional procurement — when AI cuts processing and interpretation hours, do these buyers' bid processes immediately reprice the work, or can firms hold price on proprietary methods? Separately, the multi-client data-library model (TGS-style) captures value via licensing rather than labor — what share of small-firm revenue has any library/IP component?
Who owns the client relationship — the firm, or individual senior geophysicists whose interpretation credibility is personal and portable to a competitor or to the client's in-house team?

Oil majors and miners already run in-house geoscience teams with ML tooling — what stops them from insourcing the interpretation layer entirely and buying only field acquisition, and how much of small-firm revenue is interpretation vs. acquisition?

Does any regulatory or licensure gate (state geoscientist licensure, permitting sign-offs) create defensible paid demand, or is this an unlicensed technical service where AI-equipped competition prices freely?
terminal_value_question: Mature AI plausibly makes seismic/geophysical interpretation a software function embedded in data-library platforms and clients' in-house tools, while physical acquisition (crews, vessels, drones, permitting) remains a service — is a small 541360 firm's revenue base mostly the physical acquisition that survives, or the processing/interpretation layer that AI replaces outright? Name the split; scoring the code as if interpretation revenue persists is the failure mode.
special_notes: Role-mix data gap: role_mix is 4-digit 5413, dominated by engineering-services (541330) occupations — civil engineers and architects at the top are contamination. The run must rebuild the role structure around geoscientists, field crews and processors, and mark within-role fractions accordingly.

Extreme oil & gas cyclicality: receipts are SUSB 2022 — a cyclical-recovery year after the 2020 collapse gutted seismic contracting. Do not treat single-year revenue or margins as steady-state; check EIA exploration capex trend before scoring M.

Size-class oddity in the record: the 500+ employee class (16 firms) shows LOWER avg revenue per firm ($33.9M) than the 100-499 class ($39.4M) — likely multinationals whose US-classified receipts are a fraction of global operations. n_band = 78 is small and concentrated in the 20-99 class; the roll-up target pool here is thin.

Boundary confusion: non-geophysical surveying/mapping is 541370; oil & gas field support (including some seismic acquisition) sits in 213112; environmental consulting is 541620. Industry surveys of 'geospatial' firms usually mean 541370; energy-services data often bundles this code into oilfield services. Check what a source actually covers.

Pricing-model deflation candidate: competitive project bidding to sophisticated procurement departments passes AI processing savings to clients quickly — model in pi_dist; the exception is proprietary multi-client data libraries, which small firms mostly lack.
